Text
A game about what you carry

COAT
CHECK

A game about what you carry.

Facilitator / Clinician note

This game is grounded in Neurocontextual Systems Therapy (NST; Morrison, 2026). It is designed for teens 13–18, neuroinclusive with centered representation of neurodivergent experience. The calibration sequence that follows is itself the first psychoeducational moment — the act of being asked these questions teaches that internal state has texture and that it matters. Give the player time with it. Do not rush the calibration.

Before we start

Hey.

So here's what this is. You're going to move through a day. A pretty normal day, actually. Not a crisis. Not a movie day. Just the kind of day that happens over and over and takes more out of you than it probably should.

You're going to make choices, and the choices are going to feel familiar. Some of them might feel a little too familiar.

There's no right way to play this. There's no character you're supposed to be. You're just going to be honest about what you'd actually do — or what someone you know would actually do — and the game is going to show you something about that.

At the end, it has a name for it.

But first. Before the day starts.
How are you walking in the door today.

Facilitator note

"What you'd actually do — or what someone you know would actually do" is intentional. It gives players permission to play adjacently if the content is too close to home. This is clinically important for the age group. Do not correct this framing if a player uses it.

Before the day starts

Meet the voices in your head.

Not the scary kind. Everyone has these. They're the parts of you that help you read a room, plan your next move, protect yourself, or just — tell you what you actually want. Throughout the day, you'll hear from all four.

The Feeler

I'm the one who notices things in your body before your brain has words for them — the tightness in your chest, the buzz before a conversation, the drop in your stomach when something's off. I'm reading the room emotionally, all the time.

The Fixer

I'm the strategist. I scan for risk, figure out the safest move, and keep things running smoothly. I'm not trying to control you — I'm trying to keep you okay. Sometimes that costs more than it should.

The Critic

I replay things. I notice every small mistake and run it back afterward looking for what went wrong. I know I'm exhausting. But I started out trying to help — to make sure nothing bad happened twice. I haven't fully let that go.

The Real One

I'm the quietest one. I know what you actually want — not the safe version, not the version that keeps everyone comfortable. Just the true thing. I don't always get heard. But I'm always here.

You'll hear from all of them today. They don't always agree.

Facilitator note

These four voices map loosely to internal family systems (IFS) parts language and cognitive models of self-monitoring, but are intentionally unnamed as clinical categories. Teens often immediately identify one voice as dominant. Pause here: "Which one sounds most like the voice you hear the most?" This can open the session significantly.

Quick check-in

Before we begin.

Three quick questions. No right answers — just honest ones.

I can tell when I'm acting differently than I actually feel.
I understand why some social situations cost me more than others.
I know what I need after a hard social day.
Starting capacity 10 / 10

Body

Before the day starts, check in with your body. Not your feelings about your body. Not what you think you should feel. Just — what's actually going on in there right now. Including whatever yesterday left behind.

Pick everything that's true. More than one thing can be true at the same time. The game already knows that.

How rested are you?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
How does your body feel right now?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
How hungry are you?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Facilitator note — Body calibration

The body questions establish baseline physical capacity per the NST capacity-as-variable framework. Players who select depletion indicators (barely slept, wired, forgot again) begin the game with reduced Starting Capacity — which means the same social situations cost more. This is not punishment; it is accuracy. At the end of the game, the player can see that the same day costs different amounts depending on what they brought into it. That is the lesson. Do not explain this before the game — let the mechanic teach it.

Starting capacity 10 / 10
Check in — 2 of 4

Head

This one's about what's going on up there. Not just your mood — the whole situation in your head. The static, the thing you're carrying, the part of you that's already somewhere else.

Same deal. Pick everything that's true.

How's your focus right now?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
How's your mood sitting right now?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Is anything already taking up space before the day starts?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Facilitator note — Head calibration

The head calibration surfaces cognitive and emotional capacity variables that affect social functioning. "Hyperfocused on one specific thing, which is its own problem" normalizes ADHD-pattern hyperfocus without labeling it. "It's complicated. Multiple things at once." explicitly validates co-occurring states. "There's a lot of things and I'm actively pretending there isn't" may be the most clinically significant single answer in this screen — note if selected.

Starting capacity 10 / 10
Check in — 3 of 4

Social

Here's the thing about today. You're going to be around people. Probably a lot of them. Different groups, different vibes, different versions of what they need from you. That's just the day.

Before all of that starts — where are you with it?

How's your social battery right now?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
How are you feeling about people today?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Is there anyone specific already on your mind today?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Facilitator note — Social calibration

"Desperately wanting connection and also dreading it. Both." is the option that most clearly represents the neurodivergent social experience — the simultaneous drive toward and away from connection. If selected, this is worth a dedicated conversation. "Performing ready, actually not ready" introduces the masking frame before the word masking has appeared. "Someone I said something to recently that I'm still reviewing" — if selected — is likely The Critic running. Note both.

Starting capacity 10 / 10
Check in — 4 of 4

The Real One

These aren't about how you're doing. They're about who you are when nobody's asking you to be anything in particular.

There are no wrong answers here. There are no right ones either. There's just the true ones. Pick as many as fit.

You'll hear some of these voices today
The Feeler

"Something's already off in the group before anyone says a word. You can feel it."

The Fixer

"Three people by the lockers. Standard formation. You have about six seconds before this gets complicated."

The Critic

"Wait — did you say that right? It felt like too much. Or maybe not enough. Or — okay, you're doing it again."

The Real One

"You actually wanted to tell them about the thing. That was the whole reason you came early."

When you're actually comfortable somewhere — like genuinely, not performing comfortable — what does that usually look like?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
What tends to take up space in your head when nothing else is demanding it?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
When you imagine a day that actually felt like you — what made it that way?
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Pick the things that sound most like you. Take your time with this one.
Multiselect — pick all that apply
Facilitator note — The Real One calibration

This screen generates The Real One's voice thread for the entire game. The player's selections here directly shape which authentic moments the game surfaces and how The Real One speaks throughout Act 1. In facilitated mode, review these answers with the player after the game — not before. The question "I'm a different version of myself in different rooms and I'm not sure which one is real" is often the most resonant for neurodivergent teens who have been masking since early childhood. If selected, this is a central therapeutic thread. The voice preview above introduces the four voices without explanation — the player will meet them properly in play.

The Real One

Always present
Today you're carrying

Some of it you chose. Some of it just showed up whether you wanted it or not.

Either way — it's yours today. You're walking in with all of it.

Let's see what the day does with it.

Facilitator — Pre-game pause point

Before the player hits Start the Day, this is a natural pause point for facilitated mode. You now have their full calibration constellation. Opening question: "Before we go into the day — is any of that worth naming out loud together?" This is not required. Some players will want to move straight in. Follow their lead.

Calibration summary is available in the facilitator panel. Starting Capacity has been calculated from their responses and will shape voice density and coat weight accumulation throughout Act 1.

Act 3

The coats weren't wrong.

What each coat was protecting

Every coat had a weight. That's not a verdict on the choice — it's what adaptation costs. The weight and the wisdom of the choice are two different things.

What do you think?
Overall — how much did today cost you?
5
nothingeverything
Act 4

The voices aren't the enemy.

Every voice you heard today was running a strategy. The Fixer was reducing threat. The Critic was running the worst-case scenario before it could happen to you. The Feeler was tracking what was actually going on. The Real One was holding what you actually wanted. None of them are wrong. All of them are working very hard.

Tap each voice to see what it was protecting
From your day — whose voice was this?
Did the coat fit?

For each coat you wore today — did it feel like you, or did the environment require it?

Act 5

Here are some words for it.

What happened today has a name. More than one. Having language for something doesn't fix it — but it means you're not carrying it alone and unnamed, which costs more than people realize.

The thing you put down
The Real One

You wrote that on the bus home. It has a name now — it's part of what today was. You don't have to do anything with it. But it's not just inside you anymore.

Language you can use now
If you want to put it somewhere (optional)
Choose one to finish the sentence
or say it differently
Right now — how much of today felt like you?
5
none of itmost of it
One more check-in before you go
I can tell when I'm acting differently than I actually feel.
I understand why some social situations cost me more than others.
I know what I need after a hard social day.
Day 2

Yesterday is already in the coat.

carry-in weight from yesterday
What you're still carrying

Day 2

Before the day starts.

A quick check-in — to know what you're carrying walking in.

How full is your coat rack walking in today?
How did yesterday leave you?
Anything unresolved from yesterday?
Facilitator / Clinician note

Day 2 demonstrates accumulation across days — the core claim that masking costs compound. If playing in sequence, the teen's Day 1 coat weight seeds their Day 2 starting state automatically. If cold-starting, the three intake questions set a clinically plausible baseline. Either path lands on the same map.

Day 3

There's a sub today.

carry-in weight from yesterday
Day 3

There's a sub today.

A quick check-in before the day starts.

How full is your coat rack walking in?
How did yesterday leave you?
Facilitator / Clinician note

Day 3 is a substitute teacher day — structure collapses, social code resets. Context modifiers adjust the cost of each coat at each location. The residue mechanic carries forward from Day 2. Clinical focus: social code fluency is context-dependent. Teens who mask effectively in structured environments are often more exposed in unstructured ones.

Day 3 — Navigation map

Day 4

You're home.

carry-in weight from school
Day 4

You're home.

A quick check-in before the evening starts.

How full is your coat rack walking in the door?
How was the day?
Facilitator / Clinician note

Day 4 is a new zone — the family home evening. The social code is entirely different from school. Coats built for legibility don't map cleanly to intimacy. The residue from the school days still bleeds in, but consequence text acknowledges the mismatch. Clinical focus: the hardest masking often happens with the people who know us best.

Day 4 — Home evening

Day 4 — Home
Weight: 0
Tap a room to begin
End of Day 4
School and home.
Day by day
Day 1

Day 4 — Home
total weight

What you wore tonight
Day 5

This one is different.

No teachers. No parents. No adult running the social code. Just you and your people — or the people you're hoping become your people.

Peer events are where the coats get most expensive. Because this is the room where you actually want something.

What kind of event is it tonight?
Facilitator / Clinician note

Day 5 is a peer social event — the zone with no adult authority and no institutional scaffold. The player selects party or hangout, which changes the location pool and context framing. The social code is entirely peer-generated. Clinical focus: peer masking is often the most sophisticated and most costly because there's something genuinely at stake — connection, belonging, being chosen.

Day 5 — Peer event

Facilitator — School zone

The player can complete school locations in any order. Note which ones the player approaches first — sequencing choices often reflect comfort levels with different social environments.

What do you reach for?
Your voices weigh in

The Real One
There's something you've been carrying all day that hasn't gone anywhere.
You don't have to say it to anyone. Just put it somewhere.
This stays here. It doesn't go anywhere or get scored.
